text: Welcome to another installment of robotic hernia surgery. Today we're talking about robotic transabdominal transversus abdominis release.

This is a case of a sixty-year-old woman with a prior distal pancreatectomy, splenectomy, and cholecystectomy who had a midline laparotomy and an incisional hernia. She had a BMI of thirty-five, was a non-smoker and non-diabetic.
In reviewing her cross-sectional imaging, we identified a multifocal midline hernia with a subxiphoid component, EHS M one to three, with dimensions of ten by twenty centimeters.
Her rectus width was rather narrow and we anticipated needing to do a TAR.
The surgical team sets up the case by entering through the left upper quadrant to assess the abdomen, deciding to dock the robotic system on the patient's right side for optimal access.

The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions, primarily involving the omentum and some portions of the transverse colon within the hernia sac. The procedure progresses without complications over a span of forty-five minutes.
I would say that the only opportunity for improvement in this juncture of the operation was our initial ports being a little too medial and making it a little difficult to perform our dissection.
Following the completion of lysis of adhesions, the surgeon initiates a standard retromuscular repair, focusing on the hernia site.
The surgeon enters the retro-rectus space on the patient's left side, preparing to demonstrate key elements of the retromuscular repair procedure.
Here I'm highlighting an important element of any of these procedures is the concept of hernia sac recruitment.

In particular, early in the case, I like to preserve as much autologous tissue as possible to facilitate posterior sheath and visceral sac closure. In this particular case, we recruited this sac, but did not end up using it as we sufficiently were able to mobilize the posterior sheath and peritoneum with our bilateral TAR.

The surgeon incises the posterior lamella of the internal abdominal oblique on the left side, exposing the transversus abdominis muscle. As the dissection progresses caudally, the muscle shifts laterally, revealing the tendinous portions of the transversus abdominis.
The surgeon performs a pre-transversalis preperitoneal dissection within the retromuscular space, ensuring that no muscle tissue remains attached to the peritoneum or posterior sheath.
The surgeon performs a transversus abdominis release (TAR) using a bottoms-up approach, carefully dissecting the muscle from the underlying structures.
The posterior sheath is visible with several holes made in it, likely due to previous surgical maneuvers.

The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions around the peritoneum using sharp and thermal dissection techniques, visibly weakening the peritoneal tissue. Care is taken to preserve the posterior sheath during the operation.
The surgeon performs a transversus abdominis release, creating a classic flap to close the defect in the posterior sheath without difficulty.
The surgeon extends the dissection until the yellow retroperitoneal fat becomes visible, indicating an adequate transversus abdominis release.
The surgeon performs a subdiaphragmatic dissection in the upper abdomen on the patient's left side, addressing a hernia that extends to the xiphoid process.
The video shows a classic area of the abdominal wall with a visible fat pad situated between the diaphragm and the transversus abdominis muscle, located around the eleventh and twelfth ribs.
The surgeon demonstrates a retromuscular dissection around the level of the arcuate line, performing a bottom-up transversus abdominis release (TAR) followed by a traditional top-down TAR approach.
The surgeon extends the dissection laterally, exposing the yellow retroperitoneal fat. Several small holes are visible in the posterior sheath, which are easily repaired.

The surgeon achieves good medialization of the posterior sheaths and proceeds with subdiafragmatic dissection on the contralateral side. The dissection aims to develop a space and expose the central tendon of the diaphragm, which is visible in the surgical field.

And we've done an adequate dissection. Not depicted in this particular case is the unification of the lower portions of the dissection, though that was very straightforward and not much to learn there other than unification of that central portion.
The surgeon extends the dissection to the retroperitoneum along the lateral abdominal wall, preparing to initiate closure of the visceral sac.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 absorbable V-Loc suture to run from the bottom to the top of the defect, selecting two twelve-inch sutures based on the defect's size.
The surgeon works from the head down, re-approximating the posterior sheath, which is visible in the surgical field.
Following the closure of the posterior sheath, the surgeon proceeds with defect closure using a number one V lock suture on a GS-XXI needle.

The surgeon applies careful traction to the abdominal wall, facilitating the closure of the midline. The midline visibly comes together over several runs, and the surgeon begins tying or locking the V-lock suture along the suture line.
Great. So after completing the midline fascial closure, we'll move forward with the final steps of the operation. You can see the midline has been
The surgeon places a 30x30 barred soft mesh in a diamond configuration over the reconstructed area, fixating it centrally with Tisseel adhesive to stabilize the visceral sac.
Patient did well, but did require two nights in the hospital. Overall, did great and was able to be discharged home.
Thank you very much. I hope that was educational.
